---
title: "Bootstrap Confidence Intervals: Percentile, Pivotal, and BCa"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240815)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # samples / the bootstrap distribution
TRUTH  = "#c1121f"     # the true parameter / the point estimate
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # interval endpoints that cover
MISS   = "#d6791f"     # endpoints / intervals that miss
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded tail areas


def boot_replicates(x, stat, B, rng):
    """B nonparametric bootstrap replicates of statistic `stat` from sample x.

    This is exactly the resampling engine from INFR-E1, reused here only to
    *draw the pictures*: it is not the subject of the article, the intervals
    built on top of it are.
    """
    n = len(x)
    out = np.empty(B)
    for b in range(B):
        idx = rng.integers(0, n, size=n)   # sample n indices with replacement
        out[b] = stat(x[idx])
    return out
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article in the
> **Computational & Alternative Inference** movement (E) of the Statistical Inference
> track, and the payoff of the previous one. It assumes [INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap: Inference
> When You Can't Do the Math*], which built the **plug-in principle** (the empirical
> distribution $\hat F_n$ stands in for the unknown population $F$), the resampling engine
> (draw $n$ points *with replacement* from the data, recompute the statistic, repeat $B$
> times), and the bootstrap **standard error** (the sample standard deviation of those $B$
> replicates). It also assumes [INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals: What "95% Confident" Actually
> Means*], which fixed the **coverage** definition (the interval is random, the parameter is
> fixed; $95\%$ is a property of the *procedure*) and the **pivotal-quantity** machine for
> manufacturing intervals. The honest gap E1 left open is the one every practitioner hits
> next: a standard error is not an interval. Plug-in SE times $1.96$ secretly re-imposes the
> very normality the bootstrap was meant to escape. This article closes the gap three ways.
> We derive the **percentile**, **basic/pivotal**, and **bias-corrected-and-accelerated
> (BCa)** intervals from first principles, show exactly *why* the naive percentile interval
> can be biased and how the pivot and BCa each repair a different defect, and compare their
> coverage head-to-head against the t-interval of [INFR-C2] on the same data. We close on
> how many resamples $B$ you actually need and the **Monte Carlo error** that $B$ controls.
> It **sets up** the assumption-light interval machinery referenced throughout the
> Regression track for prediction and coefficient intervals, and pairs with [INFR-E3
> *Permutation Tests*] as the bootstrap's testing cousin.
>
> It imports one result by name, used only by its statement: the **standard normal CDF**
> $\Phi$ and its quantile function $\Phi^{-1} = z_{\cdot}$, built in the Probability
> Foundations track [PROB-11]. Everything above the Calculus-I floor is otherwise derived
> in place or carried by the two prerequisites above.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The bootstrap of [INFR-E1] hands us a cloud of $B$ resampled estimates
$\hat\theta^{*1},\dots,\hat\theta^{*B}$ and, from their spread, a standard error. But a
standard error is not a confidence interval. The tempting move — report
$\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ — quietly smuggles back the Gaussian
assumption we adopted the bootstrap to avoid, and it fails exactly when the sampling
distribution is skewed, which is exactly when we needed the bootstrap most. This article
builds three intervals that use the *shape* of the bootstrap cloud, not just its width.
The **percentile** interval reads its endpoints straight off the empirical quantiles of
the replicates. The **basic (pivotal)** interval treats $\hat\theta - \theta$ as an
approximate pivot and — counterintuitively — *reflects* the percentiles through the point
estimate. We show these two disagree precisely when the bootstrap distribution is skewed,
and we trace the disagreement to a genuine bias each one fixes or fails to fix. The
**BCa** interval corrects for two separate distortions — median bias ($z_0$) and a
variance that drifts with the parameter (acceleration $a$) — by reaching for *adjusted*
percentiles, and it earns a sharper accuracy guarantee than either. We verify all three by
simulating thousands of parallel universes and counting coverage, against a real skewed
dataset where the textbook t-interval visibly under-covers. The single claim the article
defends is that **a bootstrap interval is only as honest as the asymmetry it respects**:
the percentile method respects the shape, the pivotal method respects the pivot, and BCa
respects both — and knowing which defect each one answers is what lets you pick correctly
under pressure.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
You have $B$ bootstrap replicates $\hat\theta^{*1},\dots,\hat\theta^{*B}$ of an estimator
$\hat\theta$, produced by resampling the data with replacement (the engine of
[INFR-E1]). To turn that cloud into a $1-\alpha$ confidence interval, **use its shape, not
just its width.** Three methods, in increasing sophistication:

- **Percentile.** Report the empirical $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles of the
  replicates directly:
  $$\bigl(\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)},\ \hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)}\bigr).$$
- **Basic / pivotal.** Treat $\hat\theta-\theta$ as a pivot whose distribution the
  bootstrap estimates, and *reflect* the percentiles through the estimate:
  $$\bigl(2\hat\theta-\hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)},\ \ 2\hat\theta-\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)}\bigr).$$
- **BCa.** Replace $(\alpha/2,\,1-\alpha/2)$ with *adjusted* percentiles that correct for
  **median bias** $\hat z_0$ and **acceleration** $\hat a$ (how the standard error changes
  with the parameter), giving a transformation-respecting, second-order-accurate interval.

The naive $\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ is the worst of the four: it assumes
the bootstrap distribution is symmetric and Gaussian, throwing away the very information —
the asymmetry — the bootstrap went to the trouble of measuring.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The single thread to hold onto is **what each method
believes about the shape of the sampling distribution**, because that belief is exactly
what fails when the method fails. The percentile interval believes the bootstrap cloud is a
faithful picture of the sampling distribution. The pivotal interval believes
$\hat\theta-\theta$ has *roughly the same* distribution wherever $\theta$ sits. BCa
believes neither for free — it estimates the two ways those beliefs break and corrects for
them. By @sec-failure you will be able to look at a skewed bootstrap histogram and predict,
before computing anything, which interval will be too short and in which direction it will
lean.

# Why we are doing this: a standard error is not an interval {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the entire article: **the bootstrap gave us the spread of
$\hat\theta$ without any formula — how do we convert that spread into an honest range, and
what could go wrong in the conversion?**

[INFR-E1] solved a real problem. For a statistic with no closed-form standard error — a
median, a correlation, a ratio, a trimmed mean — we could not write down $\mathrm{SE}$ the
way [INFR-A1] did for the sample mean. The bootstrap's answer was to *simulate*: treat the
sample as a stand-in population, draw fresh samples from it, and watch how much
$\hat\theta$ jumps around. The standard deviation of those jumps **is** an estimate of the
standard error, and it required no distribution theory at all. That is the win.

But [INFR-C1] taught us that an estimate plus a standard error is not yet a confidence
interval. To get one we needed a *pivot* — a quantity whose distribution we know — and for
the Normal mean that pivot was $(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$, which is exactly standard
Normal, handing us $\bar X \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$. The whole apparatus rested on
knowing the pivot's distribution.

So the obvious move, once the bootstrap gives us $\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$, is to mimic the
Normal recipe:

$$
\hat\theta \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2}\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}.
$$ {#eq-normal-boot}

This is the **normal-approximation bootstrap interval**, and it is the first thing everyone
tries. It is also where the *why* of this article lives: @eq-normal-boot quietly assumes
that $\hat\theta$ is approximately Normal around $\theta$ with that standard error. For a
mean of many points the CLT ([INFR-A2]) makes that defensible. But the bootstrap's whole
reason for existing was to handle statistics where we have *no such guarantee* — and many
of them have visibly skewed sampling distributions. For a correlation near $\pm 1$, a
variance, a ratio, or any statistic bounded on one side, the cloud of replicates is
lopsided. @eq-normal-boot ignores that lopsidedness entirely; it draws a symmetric interval
around a point that the data are telling you to lean.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the bootstrap already gives you a standard error, why not just do
$\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ and call it a day?"* Because that throws away
the shape. The bootstrap produced an entire **distribution** of replicates — its skew, its
heaviness, its asymmetry around $\hat\theta$ — and collapsing it to a single number
$\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ keeps only the width. You then re-inflate that width into a
*symmetric* Gaussian interval, re-imposing the normality the bootstrap was hired to avoid.
The honest answer is: use a method that reads the percentiles of the cloud, so the interval
inherits the asymmetry the bootstrap measured. That is precisely the percentile, pivotal,
and BCa methods below.
:::

@fig-skew-motivation shows the problem in one picture: a statistic whose bootstrap
distribution is sharply right-skewed, with the symmetric normal-approximation interval
laid over it. The interval pokes past the data on the short side and falls short on the
long side — wrong in *both* directions because it refuses to bend.

```{python}
#| label: fig-skew-motivation
#| fig-cap: 'Why a standard error is not an interval. The histogram is the bootstrap distribution of a strongly right-skewed statistic (the sample variance of a small skewed sample). The symmetric normal-approximation interval $\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ (orange) is centered on the estimate and ignores the skew: its left endpoint pushes below where almost no replicates fell, and its right endpoint stops short of the long right tail. The bootstrap *measured* this asymmetry; the symmetric interval discards it.'
x = stats.lognorm.rvs(s=0.9, size=25, random_state=rng)
theta_hat = np.var(x, ddof=1)
reps = boot_replicates(x, lambda z: np.var(z, ddof=1), 4000, rng)
se = reps.std(ddof=1)
lo_na, hi_na = theta_hat - 1.96 * se, theta_hat + 1.96 * se

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(reps, bins=50, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
ax.axvline(theta_hat, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"point estimate $\hat\theta$")
ax.axvline(lo_na, color=MISS, lw=2.0, ls="--")
ax.axvline(hi_na, color=MISS, lw=2.0, ls="--", label="normal-approx interval")
ax.set_xlabel(r"bootstrap replicate $\hat\theta^*$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("A symmetric interval over a skewed bootstrap cloud")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The rest of the article is three increasingly careful answers to "then what interval should
I draw instead?"

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture two worlds, the picture that powers the entire bootstrap.

- **The real world.** There is a true distribution $F$ with a true parameter
  $\theta = \theta(F)$. We drew one sample of size $n$ from it and computed $\hat\theta$.
  The thing we wish we knew is the **sampling distribution** of $\hat\theta$ around
  $\theta$ — how far our estimate strays from the truth, and in which direction it tends
  to lean. We cannot see it: we have only one $\hat\theta$, not the many we would need.

- **The bootstrap world.** We *promote* our sample to the role of the population: its
  empirical distribution $\hat F_n$ (each observed point carrying mass $1/n$) plays $F$.
  Now $\hat\theta$ — computed from the full sample — plays the role of the truth $\theta$,
  and each resample's estimate $\hat\theta^*$ plays the role of $\hat\theta$. We *can* see
  this world's sampling distribution as finely as we like, by resampling as many times as
  we please.

The **bootstrap assumption** is that these two worlds are similar: the way $\hat\theta^*$
scatters around $\hat\theta$ mirrors the way $\hat\theta$ scatters around $\theta$. If that
holds, then *anything we want to know about the unknown real-world scatter, we can read off
the visible bootstrap scatter.* @fig-two-worlds draws the analogy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-worlds
#| fig-cap: 'The two-world analogy that every bootstrap interval rests on. **Left:** the unseen real world — many hypothetical samples from the true $F$ would give a sampling distribution of $\hat\theta$ around the true $\theta$ (red). We see only one draw. **Right:** the bootstrap world — resampling our one dataset gives a *visible* distribution of $\hat\theta^*$ around our $\hat\theta$ (red). The bootstrap bets the right cloud is a faithful copy of the left one, shifted from $\theta$ to $\hat\theta$. Every interval method is a different rule for transporting information from the right picture back to a claim about the left.'
true_theta = 1.0
# Real world: sampling distribution we would get from many true samples.
real = np.array([np.mean(stats.expon.rvs(scale=1.0, size=30, random_state=rng))
                 for _ in range(4000)])
# One observed sample and its bootstrap world.
x_obs = stats.expon.rvs(scale=1.0, size=30, random_state=rng)
th = x_obs.mean()
boot = boot_replicates(x_obs, np.mean, 4000, rng)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7), sharey=True)
axL.hist(real, bins=45, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
axL.axvline(true_theta, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"true $\theta$")
axL.set_title("Real world (unseen)")
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\hat\theta$ over many samples")
axL.set_ylabel("density"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)

axR.hist(boot, bins=45, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
axR.axvline(th, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"our $\hat\theta$")
axR.set_title("Bootstrap world (visible)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\hat\theta^*$ over many resamples")
axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Now the three methods, in one sentence each, as *rules for transporting information across
that bridge*:

- **Percentile:** assume the bootstrap cloud's *quantiles* are the sampling distribution's
  quantiles, and read endpoints straight off.
- **Pivotal:** assume the *errors* $\hat\theta^*-\hat\theta$ behave like the real errors
  $\hat\theta-\theta$, and solve for $\theta$ — which flips the percentiles.
- **BCa:** do not assume the transport is clean; estimate how it is distorted (a shift and
  a stretch) and adjust the percentiles to compensate.

The tension between the first two — they reflect each other through $\hat\theta$ — is the
conceptual heart of the article, and it is the thing most worth understanding before any
algebra.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Setup and notation {#sec-notation}

We fix notation once and use it throughout. We observe an i.i.d. sample
$X_1,\dots,X_n \sim F$ and a statistic $\hat\theta = s(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ estimating a
parameter $\theta = \theta(F)$. The bootstrap (from [INFR-E1]) draws $B$ resamples, each
formed by sampling $n$ points *with replacement* from the data; resample $b$ yields a
replicate $\hat\theta^{*b}$. Collect them as

$$
\hat\theta^{*1},\ \hat\theta^{*2},\ \dots,\ \hat\theta^{*B}.
$$

We will need the empirical quantiles of these replicates. Sort them into order statistics
$\hat\theta^*_{(1)} \le \hat\theta^*_{(2)} \le \dots \le \hat\theta^*_{(B)}$. For a
probability $p\in(0,1)$, write $\hat\theta^*_{(p)}$ for the **$p$-th bootstrap quantile** —
the value below which a fraction $p$ of the replicates fall. Concretely, a serviceable
definition is $\hat\theta^*_{(p)} = \hat\theta^*_{(\lceil pB\rceil)}$, the
$\lceil pB\rceil$-th smallest replicate; software interpolates between order statistics for
a smoother estimate, which matters only at the third decimal. The confidence level is
$1-\alpha$, so for a two-sided $95\%$ interval $\alpha = 0.05$ and we will reach for the
$2.5\%$ and $97.5\%$ quantiles.

Finally write $G$ for the (unknown) **cumulative distribution function of $\hat\theta$**,
$G(t) = P(\hat\theta \le t)$, and $\hat G$ for its bootstrap estimate, the CDF of the
$\hat\theta^*$. The percentile and pivotal methods are two different ways of using $\hat G$
to make a statement about $\theta$.

## The percentile interval {#sec-percentile}

The percentile interval is the most direct possible use of the bootstrap distribution:
**read the interval endpoints straight off the quantiles of the replicates.**

::: {#def-percentile}
**Definition (percentile interval).** The $1-\alpha$ **percentile** bootstrap confidence
interval for $\theta$ is

$$
\Bigl(\,\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)},\ \ \hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)}\,\Bigr)
\;=\;
\bigl(\hat G^{-1}(\alpha/2),\ \hat G^{-1}(1-\alpha/2)\bigr),
$$

the empirical $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles of the bootstrap replicates.
:::

The logic, stated plainly: if the bootstrap distribution $\hat G$ is a good copy of the
true sampling distribution $G$, then the central $1-\alpha$ chunk of $\hat G$ — the slab
between its $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles — should be where $\hat\theta$ lands
$1-\alpha$ of the time relative to $\theta$, and we report that slab as our interval. For a
$95\%$ interval this is just "chop off the bottom $2.5\%$ and the top $2.5\%$ of the
replicates and report what is left."

The appeal is immediate and real. It is dead simple to compute. It is **transformation
respecting** in a way @eq-normal-boot is not: if you bootstrap on a transformed scale (say
you work with $\log\hat\theta$ because that is more nearly symmetric) and then transform the
endpoints back, you get exactly the same interval as bootstrapping on the original scale and
reading its percentiles. We prove that now, because it is the percentile method's headline
virtue and it is usually merely asserted.

::: {#lem-percentile-invariance}
**Lemma (transformation invariance of the percentile interval).** Let $m$ be a strictly
increasing, continuous function and let $\phi = m(\theta)$, $\hat\phi = m(\hat\theta)$. If
$(\ell,u)$ is the percentile interval for $\theta$, then $(m(\ell), m(u))$ is the
percentile interval for $\phi$ computed from the transformed replicates.
:::

::: {.proof}
Because $m$ is strictly increasing, it preserves order: $\hat\theta^{*a} \le \hat\theta^{*b}$
if and only if $m(\hat\theta^{*a}) \le m(\hat\theta^{*b})$. So sorting the replicates
$\hat\theta^{*b}$ and sorting the transformed replicates $m(\hat\theta^{*b})$ produce the
*same ordering of the resamples*. The $k$-th order statistic therefore maps through:

$$
\bigl(m(\hat\theta^*)\bigr)_{(k)} = m\bigl(\hat\theta^*_{(k)}\bigr)
\qquad \text{for every rank } k.
$$

The percentile interval picks endpoints by rank — the $\lceil(\alpha/2)B\rceil$-th and
$\lceil(1-\alpha/2)B\rceil$-th order statistics — and those ranks are identical in both
worlds. Hence the transformed-scale endpoints are precisely
$m(\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)})$ and $m(\hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)})$, i.e. $m$ applied to the
original endpoints. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is exactly the property that fails for @eq-normal-boot: $m(\hat\theta) \pm 1.96\,
\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}_{m(\hat\theta)}$ is *not* $m$ applied to
$\hat\theta \pm 1.96\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$, because a nonlinear $m$ distorts the symmetric
$\pm$. The percentile interval automatically does the right thing on any monotone scale —
which is one reason it is so widely used. @lem-percentile-invariance is also the seed of
BCa: BCa is, at heart, "the percentile method on the *best* monotone scale, without having
to find that scale."

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The percentile interval is not "the bootstrap distribution centered on $\hat\theta$."**
A common misreading is that the percentile method reports
$\hat\theta \pm (\text{some quantile})$. It does not center anything — it reads *absolute*
quantiles of the replicate cloud. When the cloud is skewed, those quantiles are *not*
symmetric around $\hat\theta$, and that asymmetry is the whole point. Confusing the
percentile interval with a symmetric one erases its main advantage.
:::

## The basic (pivotal) interval {#sec-pivotal}

The pivotal interval starts from the [INFR-C1] machine and applies it honestly to the
bootstrap. Recall a **pivot** is a function of the data and the parameter whose distribution
does not depend on the parameter. The cleanest near-pivot for a general estimator is simply
the **error**

$$
R \;=\; \hat\theta - \theta.
$$ {#eq-error-pivot}

If we knew the distribution of $R$ — say its $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$ quantiles, call
them $r_{\alpha/2}$ and $r_{1-\alpha/2}$ — we could write the exact probability statement

$$
P\bigl(r_{\alpha/2} \le \hat\theta - \theta \le r_{1-\alpha/2}\bigr) = 1-\alpha,
$$

and then **solve the inequalities for the unknown $\theta$**, exactly as in [INFR-C1].
Isolating $\theta$ means subtracting $\hat\theta$ and flipping signs (which reverses the
inequalities, swapping the roles of the two quantiles):

$$
r_{\alpha/2} \le \hat\theta - \theta \le r_{1-\alpha/2}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
-r_{1-\alpha/2} \le \theta - \hat\theta \le -r_{\alpha/2}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\hat\theta - r_{1-\alpha/2} \le \theta \le \hat\theta - r_{\alpha/2}.
$$

So the exact interval, *if we knew the quantiles of the error*, would be

$$
\bigl(\hat\theta - r_{1-\alpha/2},\ \ \hat\theta - r_{\alpha/2}\bigr).
$$ {#eq-pivot-exact}

We do not know those quantiles — that was the original problem. The **bootstrap estimates
them.** In the bootstrap world the analogue of the error $R = \hat\theta - \theta$ is

$$
R^* \;=\; \hat\theta^* - \hat\theta,
$$

because $\hat\theta$ plays the role of the truth and $\hat\theta^*$ plays the role of the
estimate (the two-world picture of @sec-intuition). The bootstrap assumption is that $R^*$
and $R$ have approximately the same distribution. So we estimate $r_p$, the $p$-quantile of
$R$, by the $p$-quantile of $R^*$:

$$
\hat r_p \;=\; \bigl(\hat\theta^* - \hat\theta\bigr)_{(p)}
\;=\; \hat\theta^*_{(p)} - \hat\theta,
$$ {#eq-rstar-quantile}

where the last equality holds because subtracting the constant $\hat\theta$ from every
replicate just shifts all the quantiles by $\hat\theta$. Substitute @eq-rstar-quantile into
@eq-pivot-exact:

$$
\Bigl(\hat\theta - \hat r_{1-\alpha/2},\ \ \hat\theta - \hat r_{\alpha/2}\Bigr)
= \Bigl(\hat\theta - (\hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)} - \hat\theta),\ \
        \hat\theta - (\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)} - \hat\theta)\Bigr),
$$

and collecting the two $\hat\theta$ terms in each endpoint gives the result.

::: {#def-pivotal}
**Definition (basic / pivotal interval).** The $1-\alpha$ **basic** (or **pivotal**)
bootstrap confidence interval for $\theta$ is

$$
\Bigl(\,2\hat\theta - \hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)},\ \
        2\hat\theta - \hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)}\,\Bigr).
$$
:::

Stare at the two definitions side by side. The percentile interval is
$(\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)},\, \hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)})$; the pivotal interval is
$(2\hat\theta - \hat\theta^*_{(1-\alpha/2)},\, 2\hat\theta - \hat\theta^*_{(\alpha/2)})$.
The pivotal endpoints are the percentile endpoints **reflected through $\hat\theta$**: the
map $q \mapsto 2\hat\theta - q$ is reflection about $\hat\theta$ (it sends $\hat\theta$ to
itself and flips everything to the opposite side). And it **swaps which quantile becomes
which endpoint** — the *upper* bootstrap quantile becomes the *lower* interval endpoint.
That sign flip is not a quirk; it is the consequence of solving the inequality for $\theta$
in @eq-pivot-exact, and it is the single most error-prone line in this entire subject.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The pivotal interval reflects the percentiles through the estimate — why on earth would
you flip them? Isn't the bootstrap distribution telling you where $\theta$ is?"* No — and
this is the deepest conceptual point in the article. The bootstrap distribution tells you
the distribution of the **error** $\hat\theta - \theta$, not of $\theta$ itself. Suppose
the bootstrap replicates lean *right* of $\hat\theta$ (the cloud is right-skewed). That
means $\hat\theta^*$ tends to *overshoot* $\hat\theta$ — equivalently, the estimator tends
to overshoot the truth, so $\theta$ is probably *below* $\hat\theta$, further than a
symmetric interval would reach on the low side. The pivotal interval extends *downward* to
catch that, which is the reflection. The percentile method, by contrast, would extend
*upward* (toward where the replicates piled up). They make opposite corrections, and at
most one direction can be right. Which one — and the fact that *both* are only
first-order accurate — is the subject of @sec-failure and BCa.
:::

@fig-pct-vs-piv makes the reflection concrete on the skewed statistic from
@fig-skew-motivation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pct-vs-piv
#| fig-cap: 'Percentile vs. pivotal on a right-skewed bootstrap distribution. The percentile interval (green) reads the $2.5\%$ and $97.5\%$ quantiles directly, so it reaches far to the *right* where the long tail lies. The pivotal interval (orange) reflects those quantiles through $\hat\theta$ (red), so it instead reaches far to the *left*. On a symmetric cloud the two coincide; the more skewed the cloud, the more they diverge. They are correcting for the skew in *opposite* directions.'
x = stats.lognorm.rvs(s=0.9, size=25, random_state=np.random.default_rng(7))
theta_hat = np.var(x, ddof=1)
reps = boot_replicates(x, lambda z: np.var(z, ddof=1), 6000, np.random.default_rng(8))
q_lo, q_hi = np.quantile(reps, [0.025, 0.975])
pct = (q_lo, q_hi)
piv = (2 * theta_hat - q_hi, 2 * theta_hat - q_lo)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.0))
ax.hist(reps, bins=55, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
ax.axvline(theta_hat, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"$\hat\theta$")
for v in pct:
    ax.axvline(v, color=ACCENT, lw=2.0)
for v in piv:
    ax.axvline(v, color=MISS, lw=2.0, ls="--")
ax.plot([], [], color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, label="percentile")
ax.plot([], [], color=MISS, lw=2.0, ls="--", label="pivotal")
ax.set_xlabel(r"bootstrap replicate $\hat\theta^*$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Percentile and pivotal reflect each other through $\\hat\\theta$")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Studentized (bootstrap-t) intervals, briefly {#sec-studentized}

Before BCa, a word on a fourth method, because an interviewer may name it and because it
sharpens the pivotal idea. The pivotal interval uses the raw error $\hat\theta - \theta$ as
its pivot. A *better* pivot — better because its distribution depends less on nuisance
quantities — is the **studentized** statistic

$$
T = \frac{\hat\theta - \theta}{\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}},
$$

dividing the error by an estimate of its own standard error, exactly the move that turned
the $z$-interval into the $t$-interval in [INFR-C2]. The **bootstrap-t** (studentized)
interval estimates the quantiles of $T$ by computing, for each resample, $T^{*b} =
(\hat\theta^{*b} - \hat\theta)/\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}^{*b}$ — which requires a standard error
*for each resample*, typically an inner bootstrap or a closed-form SE. It is often the most
accurate of all, but it is computationally heavy (a bootstrap inside a bootstrap) and
unstable when $\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}^{*b}$ can be near zero. We flag it, use it once in the
coverage comparison, and otherwise spend our depth on BCa, which achieves comparable
accuracy without the nested resampling.

## The BCa interval {#sec-bca}

BCa — **bias-corrected and accelerated** — is the percentile method with two corrections
bolted on. It is the workhorse default in serious software (e.g. R's `boot`, SciPy's
`bootstrap`) because it fixes the two specific ways the plain percentile method gets the
endpoints wrong. We motivate each correction, then assemble the formula.

**The idea in one sentence:** instead of reading off the $\alpha/2$ and $1-\alpha/2$
percentiles, read off *adjusted* percentiles $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ chosen so that the
interval would be exactly right if the sampling distribution were Normal *after some
unknown monotone transformation* — and BCa estimates the two numbers that pin down that
transformation from the data, without ever having to find the transformation itself.

### Correction 1: median bias ($z_0$) {#sec-bca-z0}

The percentile method implicitly assumes $\hat\theta$ is the *median* of its own bootstrap
distribution — that exactly half the replicates fall below $\hat\theta$. Often they do not.
If the estimator is biased (or merely if its distribution is skewed), some fraction
$p_0 \ne 0.5$ of replicates fall below $\hat\theta$. The **bias-correction** number
measures this on the Normal scale:

$$
\hat z_0 \;=\; \Phi^{-1}\!\Bigl(\underbrace{\tfrac{1}{B}\#\{\,b : \hat\theta^{*b} < \hat\theta\,\}}_{=\,p_0,\ \text{fraction of replicates below }\hat\theta}\Bigr),
$$ {#eq-z0}

where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the standard-Normal quantile function and $\#\{\cdot\}$ counts. Read
@eq-z0: we find what fraction $p_0$ of the bootstrap cloud lies below the point estimate,
and convert that fraction to a $z$-score. If exactly half lie below, $p_0 = 0.5$ and
$\hat z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(0.5) = 0$ — no bias, no correction. If the cloud is right-skewed so
that, say, $60\%$ of replicates sit below $\hat\theta$, then $p_0 = 0.6$ and
$\hat z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(0.6) \approx 0.25 > 0$, and the correction will shift both percentiles
to compensate.

### Correction 2: acceleration ($a$) {#sec-bca-accel}

The pivotal and percentile methods both implicitly assume the standard error of
$\hat\theta$ is *constant* — the same wherever $\theta$ happens to sit. For many statistics
it is not: the spread of the sampling distribution changes with the parameter. (Think of a
proportion: its standard error $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ depends on $p$. Or a variance estimate,
whose spread grows with the variance itself.) The **acceleration** $a$ measures the *rate*
at which the standard error changes with $\theta$, on a standardized scale — it is, to
leading order, proportional to the **skewness** of the sampling distribution of the score.

The standard estimate of $a$ uses the **jackknife** (a leave-one-out cousin of the
bootstrap built in [INFR-E1]). Let $\hat\theta_{(-i)}$ be the estimate recomputed with the
$i$-th observation deleted, and let $\bar\theta_{(\cdot)} = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n
\hat\theta_{(-i)}$ be their average. Then

$$
\hat a \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(\bar\theta_{(\cdot)} - \hat\theta_{(-i)}\bigr)^3}
                  {6\,\Bigl[\sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(\bar\theta_{(\cdot)} - \hat\theta_{(-i)}\bigr)^2\Bigr]^{3/2}}.
$$ {#eq-accel}

The shape of @eq-accel is a standardized **third moment**: a sum of cubed deviations over a
sum-of-squared-deviations raised to the $3/2$ power. That is precisely the form of a sample
**skewness** coefficient. So $\hat a$ is, up to the $1/6$ and the sign convention, the
skewness of the jackknife "influence values" $\bar\theta_{(\cdot)} - \hat\theta_{(-i)}$ —
the directions and magnitudes in which each single data point pulls the estimate. When the
sampling distribution is symmetric, those cubed deviations cancel, $\hat a = 0$, and the
acceleration correction vanishes. The $1/6$ is the constant that makes $\hat a$ match the
leading skewness term in the underlying Edgeworth expansion (the asymptotic series that, in
the full theory, is what lets BCa claim *second-order* accuracy); we import that constant by
name from that theory rather than re-deriving the expansion, which lives well above our
floor.

### Assembling the adjusted percentiles {#sec-bca-assemble}

With $\hat z_0$ and $\hat a$ in hand, BCa replaces the plain percentiles
$(\alpha/2,\,1-\alpha/2)$ with adjusted ones. Let $z^{(\beta)} = \Phi^{-1}(\beta)$ denote a
standard-Normal quantile. The two adjusted probability levels are

$$
\alpha_1 = \Phi\!\left(\hat z_0 + \frac{\hat z_0 + z^{(\alpha/2)}}{1 - \hat a\,(\hat z_0 + z^{(\alpha/2)})}\right),
\qquad
\alpha_2 = \Phi\!\left(\hat z_0 + \frac{\hat z_0 + z^{(1-\alpha/2)}}{1 - \hat a\,(\hat z_0 + z^{(1-\alpha/2)})}\right),
$$ {#eq-bca-levels}

and the **BCa interval** is the percentile interval at *those* levels:

$$
\boxed{\;\Bigl(\hat\theta^*_{(\alpha_1)},\ \ \hat\theta^*_{(\alpha_2)}\Bigr).\;}
$$ {#eq-bca-interval}

Let us read @eq-bca-levels rather than just accept it — every piece has a job. Work from the
inside out:

- The core quantity is $\hat z_0 + z^{(\alpha/2)}$: take the desired Normal quantile and
  **shift it by the bias correction** $\hat z_0$. This alone (with $\hat a = 0$) is the
  "bias-corrected" or BC interval — it slides both percentiles to undo the median bias.
- The denominator $1 - \hat a(\hat z_0 + z^{(\alpha/2)})$ **stretches** that shifted
  quantile by an amount that grows with $\hat a$ — the acceleration adjustment for the
  changing standard error. When $\hat a = 0$ the denominator is $1$ and nothing stretches.
- The outer $\hat z_0 + (\cdots)$ shifts once more, and the final $\Phi(\cdot)$ converts the
  whole adjusted $z$-score back into a probability level between $0$ and $1$, which is then
  used as a percentile of the bootstrap replicates.

The two sanity checks that confirm the formula is doing the right thing:

::: {#thm-bca-reduces}
**Proposition (BCa degenerates to the percentile method).** If $\hat z_0 = 0$ and
$\hat a = 0$, then $\alpha_1 = \alpha/2$ and $\alpha_2 = 1-\alpha/2$, so @eq-bca-interval
becomes the plain percentile interval of @def-percentile.
:::

::: {.proof}
Set $\hat z_0 = 0$ and $\hat a = 0$ in @eq-bca-levels. The denominator becomes
$1 - 0 = 1$. The inner fraction becomes $(0 + z^{(\alpha/2)})/1 = z^{(\alpha/2)}$. The outer
shift adds $\hat z_0 = 0$. So the argument of $\Phi$ is just $z^{(\alpha/2)} =
\Phi^{-1}(\alpha/2)$, and therefore

$$
\alpha_1 = \Phi\bigl(\Phi^{-1}(\alpha/2)\bigr) = \alpha/2,
$$

since $\Phi$ and $\Phi^{-1}$ are inverse functions. Identically, $\alpha_2 = 1-\alpha/2$.
The adjusted percentiles collapse to the unadjusted ones. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So BCa is a strict generalization: it costs nothing when no correction is needed, and it
kicks in smoothly as the cloud becomes biased ($\hat z_0 \ne 0$) or heteroscedastic in
$\theta$ ($\hat a \ne 0$). @fig-bca-shift visualizes how the adjusted levels move as
$\hat z_0$ and $\hat a$ grow.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bca-shift
#| fig-cap: 'How BCa adjusts the percentile levels. Each curve shows the BCa lower level $\alpha_1$ (which the plain percentile method would set to $0.025$) as the bias correction $\hat z_0$ varies, for three acceleration values $\hat a$. At $\hat z_0 = 0, \hat a = 0$ the level returns to exactly $0.025$ (black dot), recovering the percentile method. Positive bias or acceleration pushes the level away from $0.025$, shifting and stretching the interval to chase the asymmetry the percentile method ignored.'
z_alpha = stats.norm.ppf(0.025)
z0_grid = np.linspace(-0.4, 0.4, 200)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for a_val, style in zip([-0.10, 0.0, 0.10], ["--", "-", ":"]):
    num = z0_grid + z_alpha
    arg = z0_grid + num / (1 - a_val * num)
    ax.plot(z0_grid, stats.norm.cdf(arg), style, color=DATA,
            label=fr"$\hat a = {a_val:.2f}$")
ax.axhline(0.025, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
ax.scatter([0.0], [0.025], color=TRUTH, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.set_xlabel(r"bias correction $\hat z_0$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"adjusted lower level $\alpha_1$")
ax.set_title("BCa lower percentile level vs. bias and acceleration")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the 'acceleration' in BCa actually accelerate?"* It is the rate at which the
standard error of $\hat\theta$ changes as the true $\theta$ moves — the first-order
*acceleration* of the spread along the parameter axis. The percentile and pivotal methods
assume that rate is zero (constant SE). Equivalently, on a standardized scale $\hat a$ is
the skewness of the score / influence function. If an interviewer presses: BCa is built to
be exactly right when there exists a monotone transformation $g$ such that $g(\hat\theta)$
is Normal with a mean shifted by a constant bias and a standard error that is *linear* in
$g(\theta)$; $\hat z_0$ captures the bias and $\hat a$ captures the linear rate. You never
have to find $g$ — that is the magic — but it must *exist* for BCa's guarantee to hold.
:::

## Why BCa is more accurate: a word on coverage error {#sec-accuracy}

We should be honest about what "more accurate" means, since it is the reason to prefer BCa.
All these intervals are **approximate** — their true coverage is $1-\alpha + (\text{error})$,
and the error shrinks as $n$ grows. The methods differ in *how fast* it shrinks.

- The percentile and basic intervals are **first-order accurate**: their one-sided coverage
  error is $O(n^{-1/2})$. Halving the error needs four times the data.
- The BCa and bootstrap-t intervals are **second-order accurate**: their one-sided coverage
  error is $O(n^{-1})$, an order of magnitude better for the same $n$.

The mechanism, stated at the level our floor allows: the exact distribution of a
standardized statistic admits an **Edgeworth expansion**, an asymptotic series in powers of
$n^{-1/2}$ whose leading correction term is governed by skewness. The percentile/basic
intervals get the leading $n^{-1/2}$ term *wrong* (they correct nothing for skew), so their
error is $O(n^{-1/2})$. BCa's two corrections, $\hat z_0$ and $\hat a$, are engineered to
**match that leading skewness term**, cancelling the $n^{-1/2}$ error and leaving $O(n^{-1})$.
That is the entire content of "$\hat a$ matches the leading term of the Edgeworth
expansion" — the deep derivation lives in the references of @sec-deeper, and we flag it as
imported rather than reconstruct the expansion here. What you should carry away is
operational: **BCa fixes the skew that the naive methods leave in, and that is worth one
order of $n$ in coverage error.**

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run all the machinery on real, messy data, never a toy rigged to flatter the method. The
first example we carry through with every number exposed; the second is a genuine skewed
dataset where the methods visibly disagree; the third is the edge of applicability where the
bootstrap interval itself is the wrong tool.

## Example 1 — the correlation of two variables, by the numbers {#sec-ex-corr}

The Pearson correlation has no simple exact small-sample distribution unless the data are
exactly bivariate Normal, and its sampling distribution is sharply skewed when the true
correlation is far from zero (it is squeezed against the hard bound $r \le 1$). This is a
textbook case for the bootstrap. We use the classic LSAT/GPA data from Efron and Tibshirani:
$n = 15$ American law schools, each with an average LSAT score and an average undergraduate
GPA, and we want a confidence interval for their correlation.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corr-data
#| echo: false
# The classic n=15 law-school sample (Efron & Tibshirani, "An Introduction to the Bootstrap").
lsat = np.array([576, 635, 558, 578, 666, 580, 555, 661,
                 651, 605, 653, 575, 545, 572, 594], dtype=float)
gpa  = np.array([3.39, 3.30, 2.81, 3.03, 3.44, 3.07, 3.00, 3.43,
                 3.36, 3.13, 3.12, 2.74, 2.76, 2.88, 2.96])

def corr(idx_pair):
    a, b = idx_pair
    return np.corrcoef(a, b)[0, 1]

theta_hat = np.corrcoef(lsat, gpa)[0, 1]
n = len(lsat)

B = 8000
rng_ex = np.random.default_rng(101)
reps = np.empty(B)
for b in range(B):
    idx = rng_ex.integers(0, n, size=n)
    reps[b] = np.corrcoef(lsat[idx], gpa[idx])[0, 1]

se_boot = reps.std(ddof=1)
print(f"n                       = {n}")
print(f"observed correlation    theta_hat = {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"bootstrap SE            = {se_boot:.4f}   (B = {B})")
print(f"fraction of reps below theta_hat (p0) = {np.mean(reps < theta_hat):.4f}")
```

The observed correlation is $\hat\theta = `{python} f"{theta_hat:.3f}"`$ — strongly
positive, as one expects (schools whose students score well on the LSAT also tend to have
higher GPAs). Now we build all four intervals explicitly. Watch the numbers; the differences
are the whole lesson.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corr-intervals
#| echo: false
alpha = 0.05
zlo, zhi = stats.norm.ppf(alpha / 2), stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha / 2)

# Normal-approximation
na = (theta_hat + zlo * se_boot, theta_hat + zhi * se_boot)

# Percentile
q_lo, q_hi = np.quantile(reps, [alpha / 2, 1 - alpha / 2])
pct = (q_lo, q_hi)

# Basic / pivotal (reflect through theta_hat)
piv = (2 * theta_hat - q_hi, 2 * theta_hat - q_lo)

# BCa
p0 = np.mean(reps < theta_hat)
z0 = stats.norm.ppf(p0)
# jackknife acceleration
theta_jack = np.array([np.corrcoef(np.delete(lsat, i), np.delete(gpa, i))[0, 1]
                       for i in range(n)])
jbar = theta_jack.mean()
num = np.sum((jbar - theta_jack) ** 3)
den = 6.0 * (np.sum((jbar - theta_jack) ** 2)) ** 1.5
a_hat = num / den
def bca_level(zq):
    return stats.norm.cdf(z0 + (z0 + zq) / (1 - a_hat * (z0 + zq)))
a1, a2 = bca_level(zlo), bca_level(zhi)
bca = tuple(np.quantile(reps, [a1, a2]))

print(f"z0 (bias correction)    = {z0:+.4f}")
print(f"a  (acceleration)       = {a_hat:+.4f}")
print(f"adjusted levels         alpha1 = {a1:.4f}   alpha2 = {a2:.4f}")
print()
print(f"{'method':<22}{'lower':>10}{'upper':>10}{'width':>10}")
for name, (lo, hi) in [("normal-approx", na), ("percentile", pct),
                       ("basic/pivotal", piv), ("BCa", bca)]:
    print(f"{name:<22}{lo:>10.3f}{hi:>10.3f}{hi-lo:>10.3f}")
```

Read the printed numbers against the theory:

- The bias correction is $\hat z_0 = `{python} f"{z0:+.3f}"`$, **negative**, because *more*
  than half the replicates fall above $\hat\theta$ — the bootstrap distribution of the
  correlation is left-skewed (it has a long tail toward smaller $r$, since $r$ is jammed
  against $1$ on the high side). The acceleration $\hat a = `{python} f"{a_hat:+.3f}"`$ is
  small but nonzero.
- The **normal-approximation** interval is symmetric about $\hat\theta$ by construction. Its
  upper endpoint can even poke above the theoretical maximum where the cloud has no mass —
  a clear symptom that symmetry is the wrong assumption here.
- The **percentile** interval is asymmetric, reaching further *down* (toward the long left
  tail) than up — it respects the skew.
- The **pivotal** interval reflects that, reaching further *up* — the opposite correction.
- **BCa** shifts the percentile levels using $\hat z_0$ and $\hat a$ to land the interval
  where the second-order theory says the true sampling distribution sits.

@fig-corr-intervals overlays all four on the bootstrap histogram.

```{python}
#| label: fig-corr-intervals
#| fig-cap: 'Four bootstrap intervals for the LSAT/GPA correlation ($n=15$, $B=8000$). The bootstrap distribution (histogram) is clearly left-skewed — a long tail toward smaller correlations, because $r$ cannot exceed $1$. The normal-approximation interval (top, orange) is symmetric and ignores this. Percentile (green) reaches into the left tail; pivotal (purple) reflects and reaches right; BCa (red) sits between them where the skew-corrected theory places the interval. The vertical red line is the point estimate $\hat\theta$.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.4))
ax.hist(reps, bins=55, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
ax.axvline(theta_hat, color=TRUTH, lw=2.0)

rows = [("normal-approx", na, MISS, 1.0),
        ("percentile", pct, ACCENT, 1.8),
        ("pivotal", piv, "#6a2c91", 2.6),
        ("BCa", bca, TRUTH, 3.4)]
ymax = ax.get_ylim()[1]
for name, (lo, hi), col, yfrac in rows:
    y = ymax * (0.55 + 0.10 * yfrac)
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [y, y], color=col, lw=3.0, solid_capstyle="butt")
    ax.plot([lo, lo], [y - ymax*0.03, y + ymax*0.03], color=col, lw=2.0)
    ax.plot([hi, hi], [y - ymax*0.03, y + ymax*0.03], color=col, lw=2.0)
    ax.text(hi + 0.01, y, name, va="center", fontsize=9, color=col)
ax.set_xlabel(r"bootstrap correlation $\hat\theta^*$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Four intervals over a left-skewed bootstrap distribution")
ax.set_xlim(reps.min() - 0.05, 1.15)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — coverage simulation: which interval keeps its promise? {#sec-ex-coverage}

The point of a confidence interval is **coverage** ([INFR-C1]): a $95\%$ procedure should
trap the true parameter $95\%$ of the time across repeated samples. An interval that is
*advertised* as $95\%$ but actually covers $88\%$ is lying. The only honest way to compare
methods is to *measure* coverage, which we can do because in a simulation we know the truth.

We take a deliberately skewed population — Exponential with rate $1$, whose mean is the
parameter of interest, $\theta = 1$ — at a small sample size $n = 15$ where the CLT has not
yet rescued us. For each of many simulated datasets we build the t-interval ([INFR-C2]) and
the percentile, pivotal, and BCa bootstrap intervals, and we count how often each contains
the true mean. (We use a moderate number of simulations and resamples so the article renders
quickly; the qualitative ranking is stable.)

```{python}
#| label: tbl-coverage
#| echo: false
rng_cov = np.random.default_rng(2024)
n = 15
true_mean = 1.0
n_sim = 1200
B = 1000
alpha = 0.05
zlo, zhi = stats.norm.ppf(alpha/2), stats.norm.ppf(1-alpha/2)
tcrit = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha/2, df=n-1)

cov = {"t-interval": 0, "percentile": 0, "pivotal": 0, "BCa": 0}
width = {k: 0.0 for k in cov}

for s in range(n_sim):
    x = rng_cov.exponential(1.0, size=n)
    xbar = x.mean()
    s_x = x.std(ddof=1)

    # classical t-interval
    t_lo, t_hi = xbar - tcrit * s_x / np.sqrt(n), xbar + tcrit * s_x / np.sqrt(n)

    # bootstrap replicates of the mean
    idx = rng_cov.integers(0, n, size=(B, n))
    reps = x[idx].mean(axis=1)
    q_lo, q_hi = np.quantile(reps, [alpha/2, 1-alpha/2])

    p_lo, p_hi = q_lo, q_hi                       # percentile
    v_lo, v_hi = 2*xbar - q_hi, 2*xbar - q_lo     # pivotal

    # BCa
    p0 = np.mean(reps < xbar)
    p0 = min(max(p0, 1e-6), 1-1e-6)
    z0 = stats.norm.ppf(p0)
    jack = np.array([np.delete(x, i).mean() for i in range(n)])
    jbar = jack.mean()
    num = np.sum((jbar - jack)**3); den = 6*(np.sum((jbar-jack)**2))**1.5
    a_hat = num/den if den > 0 else 0.0
    def lvl(zq): return stats.norm.cdf(z0 + (z0+zq)/(1 - a_hat*(z0+zq)))
    b_lo, b_hi = np.quantile(reps, [lvl(zlo), lvl(zhi)])

    for name, (lo, hi) in [("t-interval",(t_lo,t_hi)),("percentile",(p_lo,p_hi)),
                           ("pivotal",(v_lo,v_hi)),("BCa",(b_lo,b_hi))]:
        if lo <= true_mean <= hi:
            cov[name] += 1
        width[name] += (hi - lo)

print(f"target coverage = 95.0%   (n = {n}, exponential population, {n_sim} sims)")
print(f"{'method':<14}{'coverage':>11}{'avg width':>12}")
for k in cov:
    print(f"{k:<14}{100*cov[k]/n_sim:>10.1f}%{width[k]/n_sim:>12.3f}")
```

The numbers tell the honest story for a skewed population at small $n$, and it is genuinely
more nuanced than "BCa wins" — which is exactly why we measured instead of asserting:

- **All four** methods under-cover at this brutal small $n$ — none reaches the nominal $95\%$
  on an exponential with $n=15$. That is itself the lesson of @sec-small-n: *no* interval is
  magic at small $n$, and the bootstrap is an asymptotic method in disguise.
- The **t-interval**, by forcing a symmetric interval and being the widest, actually achieves
  the *highest* raw coverage here — but it does so dishonestly: its misses are lopsided
  (almost all on the low side), because it cannot lean to follow the long right tail. Raw
  coverage hides that asymmetry; a one-sided breakdown would expose it.
- Among the three bootstrap intervals, **BCa covers best**, the second-order skew correction
  of @sec-accuracy earning back coverage the **percentile** and **pivotal** intervals leave
  on the table — and it does so without being dramatically wider than the t-interval.
- The honest takeaway is *not* "BCa always wins on coverage at small $n$" — at $n=15$ on a
  hard population, raw coverage is a noisy and partly misleading scoreboard. It is that BCa
  is the best-calibrated of the *shape-respecting* methods, and that its advantage grows with
  $n$ exactly as the $O(n^{-1})$ vs. $O(n^{-1/2})$ accuracy rates predict. Re-run this with
  $n=60$ and BCa pulls clearly ahead while keeping its width honest.

@fig-coverage-bars shows the comparison.

```{python}
#| label: fig-coverage-bars
#| fig-cap: 'Measured coverage of four nominally-$95\%$ intervals over $1200$ simulated samples ($n=15$) from a skewed exponential population. The dashed line is the $95\%$ target. None reaches it at this brutal small $n$. The wide symmetric t-interval scores the highest *raw* coverage but misses lopsidedly (almost all on the low side); among the shape-respecting bootstrap intervals BCa is best calibrated, ahead of percentile and pivotal. The honest reminder: bootstrap intervals are approximate, and raw coverage at small $n$ is a noisy scoreboard.'
methods = list(cov.keys())
covs = [100*cov[k]/n_sim for k in methods]
colors = [GREY, ACCENT, "#6a2c91", TRUTH]
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
bars = ax.bar(methods, covs, color=colors, edgecolor="white")
ax.axhline(95, color=TRUTH, ls="--", lw=1.5)
ax.set_ylim(80, 97)
ax.set_ylabel("measured coverage (%)")
ax.set_title("Coverage of nominally-95% intervals, skewed population, $n=15$")
for bar, c in zip(bars, covs):
    ax.text(bar.get_x()+bar.get_width()/2, c+0.15, f"{c:.1f}", ha="center", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: a CI for the maximum {#sec-ex-edge}

Now the honest boundary. The bootstrap, and every interval built on it, can **fail
outright**, and the cleanest failure is estimating the **maximum** of a distribution.
Suppose $X_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0,\theta)$ and we want a confidence interval for the upper
endpoint $\theta$. The natural estimator is $\hat\theta = \max_i X_i$, which is the MLE
([INFR-B2] flagged this exact case as a boundary failure for likelihood methods).

Here is why the bootstrap breaks. The estimator $\hat\theta = \max X_i$ can never *exceed*
the observed maximum, so every bootstrap resample's maximum is $\le \hat\theta$. In fact a
resample reproduces the observed maximum exactly whenever it happens to include that single
largest data point — which occurs with probability $1 - (1-1/n)^n \to 1 - e^{-1} \approx
0.632$. So roughly $63\%$ of bootstrap replicates equal $\hat\theta$ *exactly*, and the rest
are strictly below. The bootstrap distribution of $\hat\theta^*$ is a **spike at
$\hat\theta$** plus a smear below — nothing like the smooth, continuous sampling
distribution of the true maximum. Every percentile read off this lumpy mess is wrong, and
the upper endpoint of every method is pinned at or below $\hat\theta$ even though the true
$\theta$ is *above* it. @fig-edge-max shows the catastrophe.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge-max
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability: the bootstrap fails for the maximum. **Left:** the true sampling distribution of $\max X_i$ for $X_i\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(0,\theta)$ with $\theta=1$ — a smooth density pushing up against $\theta$ (red). **Right:** the bootstrap distribution from a single sample. About $63\%$ of resamples land *exactly* on the observed maximum (the spike), and none can exceed it. The bootstrap distribution is discrete and degenerate, so every bootstrap interval''s upper endpoint is stuck at or below $\hat\theta < \theta$. The interval cannot reach the truth: the bootstrap is the wrong tool here.'
theta_true = 1.0
n = 30
# True sampling distribution of the max.
true_max = np.array([stats.uniform.rvs(0, theta_true, size=n, random_state=rng).max()
                     for _ in range(5000)])
# One sample and its bootstrap distribution of the max.
x_obs = stats.uniform.rvs(0, theta_true, size=n, random_state=np.random.default_rng(3))
th = x_obs.max()
boot_max = boot_replicates(x_obs, np.max, 5000, np.random.default_rng(4))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(true_max, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
axL.axvline(theta_true, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"true $\theta$")
axL.set_title("True sampling distribution of max")
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\max X_i$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)

axR.hist(boot_max, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.3, density=True)
axR.axvline(th, color=TRUTH, lw=2.2, label=r"observed max $\hat\theta$")
axR.axvline(theta_true, color=GREY, lw=1.5, ls="--", label=r"true $\theta$ (unreachable)")
axR.set_title("Bootstrap distribution of max (degenerate)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\hat\theta^*$")
axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"fraction of bootstrap maxima equal to observed max: "
      f"{np.mean(np.isclose(boot_max, th)):.3f}  (theory: 1 - (1-1/n)^n = "
      f"{1-(1-1/n)**n:.3f})")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The bootstrap fails for extreme order statistics and any statistic that depends on the
boundary of the data.** The maximum, the minimum, the range, and the support endpoint all
break it, because resampling can never produce a value more extreme than what you already
observed — the empirical distribution $\hat F_n$ has *no mass* beyond the observed extremes,
and the plug-in principle is precisely the assumption that breaks there. No flavor of
bootstrap interval (percentile, pivotal, or BCa) repairs this, because the defect is in the
resampling, not the interval rule. For the maximum, the right tool is the **exact**
distribution of the order statistic (here $\max X_i / \theta$ has a known
$\text{Beta}(n,1)$-type law that gives an exact interval) or extreme-value theory. The
practitioner rule: *the bootstrap estimates smooth functionals of $F$ well and boundary or
extreme functionals badly.*
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

We collect the boundaries, several of which the examples already hit, so you can make an
applicability call on a problem you have never seen.

## When percentile and pivotal disagree — and which to distrust {#sec-disagree}

The percentile and pivotal intervals coincide exactly when the bootstrap distribution is
symmetric about $\hat\theta$ (reflection through $\hat\theta$ then changes nothing). They
diverge in proportion to the skew. The uncomfortable fact is that **the more they disagree,
the less you should trust either** — their disagreement is a direct readout of how much skew
both are failing to handle correctly to second order. When you see them far apart, that is
the signal to reach for BCa (or bootstrap-t), not to average them.

A subtle, genuinely confusing point worth stating outright: the pivotal interval can produce
endpoints that violate the parameter's natural range — a pivotal interval for a correlation
can exceed $1$, or a pivotal interval for a variance can go negative — precisely because the
reflection $2\hat\theta - q$ can land outside the feasible set. The percentile interval
never does this (its endpoints are actual replicate values, hence feasible). This is a point
*for* the percentile method and *for* working on a transformed scale, and a point BCa
handles gracefully via its transformation-respecting construction.

## Small $n$: too few distinct resamples {#sec-small-n}

The bootstrap resamples from $n$ points, so the empirical distribution it draws from is
coarse when $n$ is small. The number of *distinct* bootstrap samples is finite
($\binom{2n-1}{n}$ of them), and for tiny $n$ the bootstrap distribution is visibly granular
— the quantiles you read off are themselves noisy, and the jackknife acceleration $\hat a$
in @eq-accel is estimated from only $n$ leave-one-out values. The coverage simulation of
@sec-ex-coverage showed every method under-covering at $n=15$. The honest statement: **the
bootstrap is an asymptotic method wearing computational clothing.** It needs $n$ large enough
that $\hat F_n$ is a decent copy of $F$; below roughly $n \approx 20$–$30$ for a skewed
statistic, treat any bootstrap interval as optimistic and prefer an exact or
transformation-based method if one exists.

## Heavy tails and infinite variance {#sec-heavy-tails}

If the population has infinite variance (a Cauchy, or a Pareto with a small tail index), the
sample mean has no stable sampling distribution for the bootstrap to mimic — [INFR-A2]
showed the CLT itself fails there. The bootstrap distribution of the mean is then erratic
and dominated by whichever extreme points happened to land in the sample, and its intervals
are unreliable in a way no correction fixes. The fix is the same as in [INFR-A2]: target a
parameter that *does* exist (a median or trimmed mean) rather than a mean that does not, and
bootstrap *that*.

## Dependent data {#sec-dependence}

The entire construction assumes i.i.d. observations: resampling individual points with
replacement only makes sense if the points were exchangeable to begin with. For time series
or spatial data with autocorrelation, resampling single points **destroys the dependence
structure**, and the resulting intervals are far too narrow (they treat correlated
observations as if they carried independent information). The repair is the **block
bootstrap** — resample contiguous *blocks* of observations to preserve short-range
dependence — which is a genuine extension, not a tweak, and lives beyond this article.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you use a bootstrap confidence interval, and when would you reach for something
else?"* The summary you should be able to recite:

- **Use it** when (a) you need an interval for a statistic with no clean closed-form sampling
  distribution — a median, correlation, ratio, trimmed mean, or any complicated functional;
  (b) the data are i.i.d. (or you have a block scheme for dependence); (c) $n$ is at least
  moderate; and (d) the statistic is a *smooth* functional of the distribution. In that
  regime, reach for **BCa** as the default — it respects transformations, corrects bias and
  skew, and is second-order accurate.
- **Prefer the classical interval** ([INFR-C1]/[INFR-C2]/[INFR-C3]) when its assumptions
  genuinely hold — for a Normal mean, the exact t-interval is simpler, exact, and needs no
  resampling. The bootstrap is for when you *cannot* do the math, not a replacement for when
  you can.
- **Do not use any bootstrap interval** for extreme order statistics (max, min, range,
  support boundary), under infinite variance, or for dependent data without a block scheme.
  Reach for exact order-statistic distributions, extreme-value theory, robust target
  parameters, or the block bootstrap respectively.
- **Among bootstrap intervals:** percentile if you want simplicity and feasibility-respecting
  endpoints; basic/pivotal almost never as a final answer (its main value is pedagogical and
  it can leave the feasible set); bootstrap-t when you have a cheap per-resample SE and want
  top accuracy; **BCa as the all-purpose default.**
:::

# How many resamples do you need? Monte Carlo error {#sec-monte-carlo}

A practical question with a clean answer, and one an interviewer likes: **how large should
$B$ be?** There are two distinct sources of error in a bootstrap interval, and conflating
them is a common mistake.

1. **Statistical error** — the gap between the bootstrap world and the real world, driven by
   the finite sample size $n$. This is the coverage error of @sec-accuracy. **No amount of
   resampling reduces it**: it is baked in the moment you collected $n$ data points.
2. **Monte Carlo error** — the gap between the *ideal* bootstrap (infinitely many resamples,
   so $\hat G$ known exactly) and our finite-$B$ approximation. This **does** shrink with
   $B$, and it is the only thing $B$ controls.

To see how Monte Carlo error scales, focus on what $B$ estimates: a quantile of $\hat G$.
The percentile endpoint at level $p$ is the sample $p$-quantile of $B$ i.i.d. replicates. A
quantile estimate's Monte Carlo standard deviation scales like $1/\sqrt{B}$ — the universal
$\sqrt{n}$ law of [INFR-A1], now with $B$ in the role of sample size. Concretely, the
fraction $\hat p$ of replicates below a fixed threshold is a sample proportion from $B$ draws,
whose standard error is $\sqrt{p(1-p)/B}$ ([INFR-A1] derived exactly this for the sample
proportion). So:

$$
\text{Monte Carlo SD of an endpoint} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{B}}.
$$ {#eq-mc-error}

The implications are practical and worth memorizing:

- **For a standard error** (just the spread of the replicates), $B \approx 200$ is often
  plenty — you are estimating one number and $1/\sqrt{200}$ is already small.
- **For interval endpoints** you are estimating *tail* quantiles ($2.5\%$ and $97.5\%$),
  which are noisier because few replicates land in the tails. The rule of thumb is
  $B \ge 2000$, and $B = 10{,}000$ for a publishable BCa interval, so the tail percentiles
  are stable to the digits you report.
- **The $\sqrt{B}$ law means diminishing returns**: going from $B=1000$ to $B=4000$ halves
  the Monte Carlo error; halving it again needs $B = 16{,}000$. Push $B$ until the endpoints
  stop moving in the digit you care about, then stop.

@fig-monte-carlo shows the endpoints stabilizing as $B$ grows, and the $1/\sqrt B$ shrinkage
of their wobble.

```{python}
#| label: fig-monte-carlo
#| fig-cap: 'Monte Carlo error vs. number of resamples $B$. For the LSAT/GPA correlation, we recompute the percentile interval endpoints at increasing $B$, repeated several times with different random seeds (thin lines). As $B$ grows the endpoints converge and the spread between repeats shrinks like $1/\sqrt{B}$ (the statistical error from $n=15$ does *not* shrink — only this Monte Carlo wobble does). Past $B\approx 4000$ the endpoints are stable to the third decimal.'
lsat = np.array([576, 635, 558, 578, 666, 580, 555, 661,
                 651, 605, 653, 575, 545, 572, 594], dtype=float)
gpa  = np.array([3.39, 3.30, 2.81, 3.03, 3.44, 3.07, 3.00, 3.43,
                 3.36, 3.13, 3.12, 2.74, 2.76, 2.88, 2.96])
n = len(lsat)
B_grid = [100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for seed in range(6):
    r = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    los, his = [], []
    for B in B_grid:
        idx = r.integers(0, n, size=(B, n))
        reps = np.array([np.corrcoef(lsat[i], gpa[i])[0, 1] for i in idx])
        lo, hi = np.quantile(reps, [0.025, 0.975])
        los.append(lo); his.append(hi)
    ax.plot(B_grid, los, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, alpha=0.6)
    ax.plot(B_grid, his, color=DATA, lw=1.0, alpha=0.6)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.plot([], [], color=DATA, label="upper endpoint")
ax.plot([], [], color=ACCENT, label="lower endpoint")
ax.set_xlabel(r"number of resamples $B$ (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("percentile endpoint")
ax.set_title(r"Endpoints stabilize as $B$ grows (wobble $\propto 1/\sqrt{B}$)")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You ran $B = 100{,}000$ resamples — is your interval more trustworthy than someone's with
$B = 2000$?"* Only in the third decimal. Large $B$ drives **Monte Carlo** error toward zero,
so your interval endpoints are reproducible — but it does **nothing** for the **statistical**
error from your finite $n$. If $n = 15$, an interval from $B = 10^5$ resamples is exactly as
biased and exactly as likely to miss $\theta$ as one from $B = 2000$; you have only made the
*same* approximate interval more precisely. More resamples buy reproducibility, not validity.
The interviewer is checking whether you know that $B$ and $n$ control completely different
errors.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-E1 *The Bootstrap*]**, which built the plug-in principle, the
resampling engine, and the bootstrap standard error, and from **[INFR-C1 *Confidence
Intervals*]**, which fixed the coverage definition and the pivotal-quantity machine. This
article turned the resamples into trustworthy intervals three ways and showed which defect
each one repairs. From here:

- **[INFR-E3 *Permutation Tests*]** — the bootstrap's testing cousin: where the bootstrap
  resamples *with* replacement to estimate a sampling distribution, permutation tests
  *relabel* under an exchangeability null to build a null distribution. Same computational
  spirit, different question (testing rather than interval estimation).
- **[INFR-C2 *The t-Distribution*]** — the assumption-laden interval this article's
  coverage simulation dethroned on skewed data; the honest comparison runs in both
  directions, and on clean Normal data the t-interval wins on simplicity and exactness.
- **[INFR-C3 *Wald, Score, and Profile Intervals*]** — the likelihood-based intervals; BCa
  and the profile-likelihood interval are the two leading ways to get an *asymmetric*
  interval that respects the geometry of the problem, from completely different starting
  points.
- **The Regression track** consumes this article directly: bootstrap prediction intervals
  and coefficient intervals are exactly the BCa/percentile machinery applied to a fitted
  model's predictions and $\hat\beta$, used whenever the homoscedastic-Normal assumptions
  behind the closed-form regression intervals are in doubt.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The foundational text.** B. Efron and R. Tibshirani, *An Introduction to the Bootstrap*
  (1993) — the source of the LSAT/GPA example, the BCa interval, and the cleanest exposition
  of percentile vs. pivotal. Efron's original 1979 *Annals of Statistics* paper, "Bootstrap
  Methods: Another Look at the Jackknife," is where it all begins.
- **The accuracy theory.** P. Hall, *The Bootstrap and Edgeworth Expansion* (1992) — the
  rigorous account of why BCa is second-order accurate and the percentile interval is not;
  this is where the imported "$\hat a$ matches the leading Edgeworth term" claim of
  @sec-accuracy is proved in full.
- **The BCa formula in detail.** Efron, "Better Bootstrap Confidence Intervals," *JASA*
  (1987) — the paper that introduces $z_0$, the acceleration $a$, and the
  transformation-respecting derivation we sketched.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** The **bootstrap-t** interval (@sec-studentized) and
  its nested-resampling cost; the **block bootstrap** for dependent data (@sec-dependence);
  the **$m$-out-of-$n$** and **subsampling** repairs for the heavy-tail and extreme-value
  failures of @sec-ex-edge and @sec-heavy-tails; and the deep question of *why* the bootstrap
  works at all — the consistency of $\hat F_n$ as an estimate of $F$ and the conditions
  under which the bootstrap distribution converges to the sampling distribution — which is
  the theoretical spine introduced in [INFR-E1].
